lessons.title: The Roman Republic
lessons.subtitle: From Kingdom to Republic — The Birth of Roman Liberty
lessons.overview:
In 509 BC, the Roman people overthrew their last king and established a republic. This new system of government would shape Western civilization for centuries.

The Republic was built on the principle that power should be shared and checked, not concentrated in one ruler.
lessons.standard:
The Roman Republic was founded after the overthrow of King Tarquinius Superbus. It introduced a complex system of elected magistrates, most notably two consuls who shared executive power and could veto each other.

The Senate, composed of Rome's wealthiest and most influential citizens, advised the consuls and controlled state finances. Over 400+ years, Rome expanded from a small city-state to the dominant Mediterranean power.
lessons.deep:
The Roman Republic's constitution was never written in a single document. It evolved through tradition (mos maiorum), conflict between patricians and plebeians, and pragmatic responses to crises.

Key institutions included the Centuriate Assembly, Tribal Assembly, Plebeian Council, and temporary dictators. The system's checks and balances became a weakness as ambitious generals accumulated power the old institutions could no longer contain.

timeline.title: Republic Founded
timeline.description: Tarquin the Proud is expelled; first consuls take office.

timeline.title: First Secession of the Plebs
timeline.description: Creation of the Tribune of the Plebs.

timeline.title: Twelve Tables
timeline.description: Rome's first written law code.

timeline.title: Punic Wars
timeline.description: Rome defeats Carthage and dominates the west Mediterranean.

timeline.title: Caesar Crosses the Rubicon

timeline.title: Augustus
timeline.description: Empire begins.

figures.significance: Led the revolt against Tarquin. Symbol of republican virtue.

figures.shortBio: Saved Rome then returned to his farm — ideal of civic duty.
figures.significance: Saved Rome then returned to his farm — ideal of civic duty.

figures.significance: Crossed the Rubicon. Dictatorship ends the Republic.

quiz.question: In what year was the Roman Republic traditionally founded?
quiz.explanation: 509 BC marks the traditional founding of the Republic.

quiz.question: The Roman Republic had a single written constitution like the United States.
quiz.explanation: Rome never had one written constitution; it evolved through custom and struggle.

lessons.title: Ancient Egypt: The Old Kingdom
lessons.subtitle: Pyramids, Pharaohs, and the Dawn of Civilization
lessons.overview: The Old Kingdom represents the golden age of pyramid building and the consolidation of pharaonic power in Egypt.
lessons.standard: During the Old Kingdom, Egypt saw the construction of the Great Pyramids of Giza under pharaohs Khufu, Khafre, and Menkaure. The period established cultural and religious foundations that defined ancient Egypt for millennia.
lessons.deep: Centralized administration, bureaucracy, and religious ideology of the pharaoh as living god enabled monumental construction. Collapse after Pepi II's long reign led into the First Intermediate Period.

timeline.title: Old Kingdom Begins
timeline.description: Third Dynasty under Djoser; Step Pyramid at Saqqara.

timeline.title: Great Pyramid
timeline.description: Khufu builds the largest pyramid.

timeline.title: Old Kingdom Ends
timeline.description: Central authority collapses.

figures.name: Djoser
figures.shortBio: Commissioned the first pyramid complex.
figures.significance: Commissioned the first pyramid complex.

figures.name: Imhotep
figures.shortBio: Designed the Step Pyramid; later deified.
figures.significance: Designed the Step Pyramid; later deified.

figures.name: Khufu
figures.shortBio: Fourth Dynasty pharaoh.
figures.significance: Fourth Dynasty pharaoh.

quiz.question: Which pharaoh commissioned the Great Pyramid of Giza?
quiz.explanation: Khufu (Cheops) built the Great Pyramid.

lessons.title: The Indus Valley Civilization
lessons.subtitle: Planned cities, seals, and a script we still cannot fully read
lessons.overview: Along the Indus and its tributaries rose one of the world's earliest urban civilizations — contemporary with Egypt and Mesopotamia, yet distinct in its orderly cities and almost invisible kings.
lessons.standard:
Major sites include Harappa and Mohenjo-daro: grid streets, baked-brick houses, covered drains, and the Great Bath. Trade stretched to Mesopotamia. Thousands of steatite seals show animals and a still-undeciphered script.

Unlike Egypt's pharaohs or Mesopotamia's kings, Indus society left few clear royal monuments — leading scholars to debate how power was organized.
lessons.deep:
Urban planning suggests strong civic norms: standardized brick ratios, water management, and craft specialization (beads, metallurgy, pottery). The civilization declined after c. 1900 BC — climate shifts, river course changes, and trade disruption are leading theories.

The undeciphered script (short inscriptions) remains one of archaeology's great puzzles. Continuities with later South Asian culture are debated carefully; avoid simplistic 'direct lineage' claims.

timeline.title: Early Harappan
timeline.description: Village networks densify toward urbanism.

timeline.title: Mature Harappan
timeline.description: Great cities at peak organization and trade.

timeline.title: Transformation
timeline.description: Urban centers fade; regional cultures emerge.

timeline.title: Modern Discovery
timeline.description: Excavations reveal Harappa and Mohenjo-daro to the world.

figures.name: John Marshall
figures.shortBio: Director-General of the ASI; announced the civilization publicly in the 1920s.
figures.significance: Director-General of the ASI; announced the civilization publicly in the 1920s.

figures.name: R. D. Banerji
figures.shortBio: Early work at Mohenjo-daro helped identify the culture.
figures.significance: Early work at Mohenjo-daro helped identify the culture.

figures.name: Dayaram Sahni

figures.significance: Key early excavations at Harappa.

quiz.question: Which feature is most associated with Mature Harappan cities?
quiz.explanation: Harappan cities are famous for planned streets and sophisticated drainage.

quiz.question: The Indus script has been fully deciphered like Egyptian hieroglyphs.
quiz.explanation: The Indus script remains undeciphered.

lessons.title: Ashoka and the Mauryan Empire
lessons.subtitle: From bloody conquest to dhamma on stone
lessons.overview: The Mauryan Empire was the first to unite most of the Indian subcontinent under one power. Emperor Ashoka's reign became legendary — not only for scale, but for a public turn toward ethics after war.
lessons.standard:
Chandragupta Maurya founded the empire with help of Chanakya (Kautilya). Bindusara expanded it; Ashoka's conquest of Kalinga (c. 261 BC) was devastating. Afterward, Ashoka promoted dhamma — moral conduct, religious tolerance, and welfare — through rock and pillar edicts across the realm.

These edicts are among the earliest extensive royal inscriptions in India and a primary source for historians.
lessons.deep:
The Arthashastra tradition (statecraft, spies, economics) illuminates Mauryan administration even if exact dating of texts is debated. Ashoka's Buddhism was personal and public, but dhamma was broader than a single sect.

After Ashoka, the empire weakened; the last Mauryan ruler was overthrown c. 185 BC. Memory of Ashoka was revived in modern India as a symbol of ethical power (Lion Capital of Sarnath).

timeline.title: Empire Founded
timeline.description: Chandragupta establishes Mauryan power.

timeline.title: Kalinga War
timeline.description: Ashoka's costly victory; turning point in royal ideology.

timeline.title: Edicts
timeline.description: Dhamma messages carved on rocks and pillars.

timeline.title: Mauryan Fall
timeline.description: Pushyamitra Shunga ends the dynasty.

figures.name: Chandragupta Maurya
figures.shortBio: Built the empire that Ashoka would inherit.
figures.significance: Built the empire that Ashoka would inherit.

figures.name: Chanakya (Kautilya)
figures.shortBio: Advisor associated with Arthashastra statecraft.
figures.significance: Advisor associated with Arthashastra statecraft.

figures.name: Ashoka
figures.shortBio: Expanded then redefined imperial purpose through dhamma.
figures.significance: Expanded then redefined imperial purpose through dhamma.

quiz.question: What major event is said to have transformed Ashoka's outlook?
quiz.explanation: Tradition and edicts link Ashoka's remorse and dhamma turn to the Kalinga conflict.

quiz.question: Ashoka's edicts are useful because they are primary written sources from his reign.
quiz.explanation: The rock and pillar edicts are key contemporary inscriptions.

lessons.title: The Silk Roads
lessons.subtitle: Not one road — a web of trade, ideas, and risk
lessons.eraOrTopic: Silk Roads • c. 2nd century BC – 15th century AD
lessons.overview: The Silk Roads were overlapping land and sea routes linking China, Central Asia, India, Persia, and the Mediterranean. Silk was famous — but religions, technologies, diseases, and stories traveled too.
lessons.standard:
Han China and empires to the west incentivized long-distance exchange. Caravanserais, oasis cities (Samarkand, Kashgar), and maritime routes via the Indian Ocean formed a network, not a highway.

Buddhism spread into Central and East Asia along these corridors. Later, Islam, paper-making knowledge, and countless goods moved in both directions.
lessons.deep:
The term 'Silk Road' is modern (coined in the 19th century). Historians prefer 'Silk Roads' to stress plurality. Political stability under empires (Han, Kushan, Abbasid, Mongol) often boosted traffic; fragmentation raised costs and dangers.

The Black Death's westward movement in the 14th century shows the dark side of connectivity. Maritime routes eventually rivaled overland ones in the age of gunpowder empires and European oceanic expansion.

timeline.title: Han Expansion West
timeline.description: Chinese engagement with Central Asia intensifies exchange.

timeline.title: Kushan Hub
timeline.description: Central Asian power bridges India, China, and Iran.

timeline.title: Abbasid Age
timeline.description: Baghdad and Central Asian cities thrive as trade nodes.

timeline.title: Pax Mongolica
timeline.description: Mongol peace lowers barriers for merchants and missionaries.

figures.name: Zhang Qian
figures.shortBio: Missions westward opened Chinese knowledge of Central Asia.
figures.significance: Missions westward opened Chinese knowledge of Central Asia.

figures.name: Xuanzang
figures.shortBio: 7th-century journey to India became a classic of travel and faith.
figures.significance: 7th-century journey to India became a classic of travel and faith.

figures.name: Ibn Battuta
figures.shortBio: 14th-century journeys mapped a connected Afro-Eurasian world.
figures.significance: 14th-century journeys mapped a connected Afro-Eurasian world.

quiz.question: The Silk Roads carried only silk and luxury goods.
quiz.explanation: Ideas, religions, technologies, crops, and diseases also moved along the network.

quiz.question: Who coined a modern name for these routes in the 19th century (commonly attributed)?
quiz.explanation: German geographer Ferdinand von Richthofen popularized 'Seidenstraße' (Silk Road).

lessons.title: Mansa Musa and the Mali Empire
lessons.subtitle: Gold, scholarship, and the most famous pilgrimage in medieval Africa
lessons.overview: In the 14th century, Mansa Musa of Mali made a pilgrimage to Mecca so lavish that chroniclers said it disrupted gold prices in Cairo. Behind the legend stood a powerful West African empire built on trade and learning.
lessons.standard:
Mali controlled key trans-Saharan routes in gold and salt. Cities like Timbuktu and Djenné became centers of commerce and Islamic scholarship. Musa's hajj (c. 1324) displayed wealth and piety and put Mali firmly on Mediterranean mental maps.

Arabic sources and later oral traditions (epics of Sundiata) help reconstruct this world — each with strengths and biases.
lessons.deep:
West African statecraft combined military power, taxation of trade, and Islamic institutions with local traditions. Manuscript cultures of Timbuktu show sophisticated law, astronomy, and theology.

European mapmakers later marked 'Rex Meli' and gold fields, shaping myths of African wealth. Modern historians stress African agency — Mali was not a periphery but a center in its own networks.

timeline.title: Sundiata
timeline.description: Foundations of Mali after victory at Kirina (tradition).

timeline.title: Mansa Musa
timeline.description: Reign at Mali's height of fame.

timeline.title: Hajj
timeline.description: Pilgrimage becomes a pan-Mediterranean story.

timeline.title: Shift
timeline.description: Songhai and changing trade routes reshape the region.

figures.name: Sundiata Keita
figures.shortBio: Hero of the Epic of Sundiata; associated with Mali's rise.
figures.significance: Hero of the Epic of Sundiata; associated with Mali's rise.

figures.name: Mansa Musa
figures.shortBio: Famous for pilgrimage, patronage, and imperial wealth.
figures.significance: Famous for pilgrimage, patronage, and imperial wealth.

figures.name: Ibn Khaldun
figures.shortBio: North African scholar whose works reference Western Sudanese states.
figures.significance: North African scholar whose works reference Western Sudanese states.

quiz.question: Mansa Musa's fame in outside sources is most tied to which journey?
quiz.explanation: His pilgrimage to Mecca was widely reported in Arabic chronicles.

quiz.question: Timbuktu was known as a center of Islamic learning as well as trade.
quiz.explanation: It became renowned for scholars, libraries, and manuscript culture.

lessons.title: 1453: Fall of Constantinople
lessons.subtitle: Cannons, walls, and the end of a thousand-year empire
lessons.overview: When Ottoman forces under Mehmed II took Constantinople, the Byzantine Empire ended. The event shocked Christendom, thrilled the Ottoman world, and became a hinge between medieval and early modern eras.
lessons.standard:
Constantinople's Theodosian Walls had defied attackers for centuries. Mehmed II brought massive cannons, a large army and fleet, and relentless engineering — including hauling ships overland into the Golden Horn.

After the city fell, it became the Ottoman capital (Istanbul). Hagia Sophia was converted into a mosque. Greek scholars' westward movement is often linked to Renaissance currents (one factor among many).
lessons.deep:
Byzantium had been shrinking for generations; 1453 was climax, not bolt from the blue. Ottoman statecraft blended Turkic, Persian, and Islamic imperial traditions with pragmatic rule over diverse peoples.

In European memory, '1453' became a symbol of civilizational shift. Historians caution against single-date myths: printing, Atlantic voyages, and gunpowder empires all reshaped the 15th–16th centuries together.

timeline.title: Fourth Crusade
timeline.description: Latins sack Constantinople; Byzantium fatally weakened.

timeline.title: Mehmed II
timeline.description: Young sultan prepares a decisive campaign.

timeline.title: Siege
timeline.description: Artillery and assaults break the city's defenses.

timeline.title: New Capital
timeline.description: Ottomans remake the city as imperial center.

figures.name: Mehmed II
figures.shortBio: Conqueror who made the city his capital.
figures.significance: Conqueror who made the city his capital.

figures.name: Constantine XI
figures.shortBio: Died in the final defense of the city.
figures.significance: Died in the final defense of the city.

figures.name: Orban
figures.shortBio: Associated with giant bombards used in the siege.
figures.significance: Associated with giant bombards used in the siege.

quiz.question: In which year did Constantinople fall to the Ottomans?
quiz.explanation: The city fell on 29 May 1453.

quiz.question: The Theodosian Walls had protected the city for centuries before 1453.
quiz.explanation: Those massive land walls were legendary defensive works.

lessons.title: Babur and the Mughal Founding
lessons.subtitle: A Timurid prince, gunpowder, and a new imperial age in India
lessons.overview: In 1526, Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi at Panipat and founded the Mughal Empire. What began as a conquest state became one of the world's great early modern empires — administration, culture, and monumental architecture.
lessons.standard:
Babur, a Timurid descendant from Central Asia, used cavalry, tulughma tactics, and gunpowder field fortifications. His memoir, the Baburnama, is a vivid primary source — frank about nature, politics, and homesickness.

Humayun lost and regained the throne; Akbar later consolidated a durable imperial system. The Mughals ruled a vast, multi-religious society through mansabdari ranks, revenue systems, and court culture.
lessons.deep:
Calling the Mughals simply 'foreign invaders' oversimplifies centuries of Indianization, alliance-building, and cultural synthesis (Persianate court culture meeting South Asian society).

Military success mattered, but longevity required bureaucracy, legitimacy, and negotiation with local elites (Rajput alliances under Akbar are classic examples). Architecture from Humayun's Tomb to the Taj Mahal (later) shows evolving imperial style.

timeline.title: First Panipat
timeline.description: Babur defeats the Lodi sultan; Mughal rule begins.

timeline.title: Humayun's Exile
timeline.description: Sur dynasty interruption; Mughal restoration.

timeline.title: Akbar
timeline.description: Expansion and administrative consolidation.

timeline.title: Taj Mahal
timeline.description: Shah Jahan's mausoleum becomes a global icon.

figures.name: Babur
figures.shortBio: Timurid prince; memoirist and conqueror.
figures.significance: Timurid prince; memoirist and conqueror.

figures.name: Humayun
figures.shortBio: Lost then recovered the empire.
figures.significance: Lost then recovered the empire.

figures.name: Akbar

figures.significance: Built the administrative and political foundations of high Mughal power.

quiz.question: The Baburnama is valuable as a first-person royal memoir.
quiz.explanation: Babur's autobiography is a major primary source for the period.

lessons.title: The French Revolution
lessons.subtitle: Rights, terror, and the remaking of politics
lessons.overview: In a decade, France went from absolute monarchy toward republic, empire, and a language of rights that still shapes global politics. The Revolution was inspiring and terrifying — often at the same time.
lessons.standard:
Fiscal crisis, Enlightenment ideas, and social inequality set the stage. 1789 brought the Estates-General, National Assembly, Bastille, and Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen.

Radicalization produced republic, war, and the Terror under Robespierre. The Directory gave way to Napoleon Bonaparte's rise (1799). Outcomes included legal reforms, nationalism, and long wars across Europe.
lessons.deep:
Historians debate causes (structure vs contingency) and character (liberal vs social revolution). Women, enslaved people in the colonies, and peasants experienced the Revolution differently than Parisian politicians.

The Haitian Revolution intertwined with French events — forcing questions about universal rights and slavery. Revolutionary calendars, metric impulses, and de-Christianization show how deeply reformers tried to redesign daily life.

timeline.title: Estates-General & Bastille
timeline.description: Crisis becomes revolution.

timeline.title: Republic & Terror
timeline.description: War and radical politics peak.

timeline.title: Brumaire
timeline.description: Napoleon seizes power.

timeline.title: Emperor

figures.significance: Monarch whose authority collapsed; executed 1793.

figures.name: Maximilien Robespierre

figures.significance: Symbol of revolutionary virtue and the Terror.

figures.name: Olympe de Gouges

figures.significance: Demanded women's rights; executed during the Terror.

quiz.explanation: Colonial revolutions, especially in Haiti, were deeply connected.

lessons.title: Partition of India, 1947
lessons.subtitle: Independence, borders, and human catastrophe
lessons.overview: In August 1947, British India was partitioned into India and Pakistan. Freedom arrived with mass migration, communal violence, and unresolved questions that still shape the subcontinent.
lessons.standard:
Long-term communal politics, colonial policies, wartime strains, and failed negotiations preceded Partition. The Mountbatten Plan accelerated transfer of power. The Radcliffe Line drew borders through Punjab and Bengal with devastating speed.

Millions crossed new frontiers; hundreds of thousands died. Kashmir and other princely-state issues became enduring conflicts. Oral histories and literature (Khushwant Singh, Saadat Hasan Manto, and many others) preserve the human texture.
lessons.deep:
No single cause explains Partition. Historians examine the two-nation theory, Congress-Muslim League rivalry, British exit strategies, and local breakdowns of order.

Studying 1947 requires care: avoid communal blame games; center evidence, multiple perspectives, and the experiences of women, refugees, and minorities. Memory politics continue in textbooks and public debate across India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh (1971).

timeline.title: Lahore Resolution

timeline.title: Cabinet Mission fails

lessons.title: How Print Changed the World
lessons.subtitle: Moveable type, pamphlets, and the explosion of ideas
lessons.overview: When moveable-type printing scaled in Europe after Gutenberg, books became faster and cheaper to multiply. Ideas could outrun censors — and start arguments that remade religion, science, and politics.
lessons.standard:
Woodblock printing existed earlier in East Asia; Korea developed moveable metal type before Gutenberg. Europe's mid-15th-century print boom still transformed scale: Bibles, indulgences, maps, and later newspapers.

The Reformation spread through pamphlets. Scientific diagrams and correspondence networks accelerated knowledge-sharing. Literacy and vernacular languages gained ground over centuries.
lessons.deep:
Print did not automatically create modernity — readers, markets, states, and religions shaped its use. Authorities used print for propaganda even as dissidents used it for resistance.

Comparing Eurasian printing histories avoids Eurocentrism: China, Korea, and the Islamic world's manuscript cultures had different incentives and trajectories. The 'print revolution' is best seen as a global, uneven story.

lessons.title: Our Solar System
lessons.subtitle: Eight Worlds, One Star, Countless Stories
lessons.overview: Our solar system formed about 4.6 billion years ago from a collapsing cloud of gas and dust. At its center burns the Sun — holding everything in its gravitational embrace.
lessons.standard: Inner terrestrial planets (Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars) and outer gas/ice giants (Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune). The asteroid belt lies between Mars and Jupiter; beyond Neptune, the Kuiper Belt and Oort Cloud.
lessons.deep: Formed from a protoplanetary disk. Close to the Sun only rock and metal condensed; farther out, ices and gases formed giants. Processes: accretion, differentiation, Late Heavy Bombardment, giant planet migration (Nice model).

timeline.title: Solar System Forms
timeline.description: Molecular cloud collapses into Sun + disk.

timeline.title: Apollo 11
timeline.description: Humans walk on the Moon.

lessons.title: Black Holes: Gravity's Ultimate Triumph
lessons.subtitle: Where spacetime ends and our understanding begins
lessons.overview: Black holes are regions of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing, not even light, can escape — extreme predictions of Einstein's general relativity.
lessons.standard: Stellar-mass black holes form when massive stars collapse. Supermassive black holes sit at the centers of most galaxies, including the Milky Way.
lessons.deep: The Event Horizon Telescope imaged M87* (2019) and Sagittarius A* (2022). Hawking radiation, the information paradox, and singularities remain active frontiers.

timeline.title: Schwarzschild Solution

timeline.title: Kerr Solution
timeline.description: Rotating black holes.
